description: NeetCode core DS concepts — presented once before the first problem of each new category

ds_concepts.title: Arrays
ds_concepts.summary: Contiguous memory. Random access O(1). The foundation of almost every algorithm.
key_concepts: Random access: arr[i] is O(1) — direct memory offset calculation
Insertion/deletion at end: O(1) amortized (dynamic arrays double capacity)
Insertion/deletion at middle: O(n) — must shift elements
Two-pass technique: scan once to gather info, scan again to act on it
Prefix sums: precompute arr[0..i] totals to answer range queries in O(1)
when_to_use: Data is ordered and you need range queries
Building a prefix sum for range aggregation
common_patterns: Prefix sums for range sum queries
Sorting to enable two-pointer / binary search
Kadane's algorithm for maximum subarray
Dutch national flag for 3-way partition
ds_concepts.code_example:
# Prefix sum — range query in O(1) after O(n) build
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
prefix = [0] * (len(nums) + 1)
for i, n in enumerate(nums):
    prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + n
# Sum of nums[1..3] = prefix[4] - prefix[1] = 10 - 1 = 9

# Kadane's — max subarray O(n)
max_sum = cur = nums[0]
for n in nums[1:]:
    cur = max(n, cur + n)
    max_sum = max(max_sum, cur)
thinking_questions: Why does inserting at the front of an array cost O(n)? What data structure fixes this?
Given an array, how would you find all pairs that sum to a target in O(n)?

ds_concepts.title: Hash Maps & Hash Sets
ds_concepts.summary: O(1) average insert/lookup/delete. Trade memory for speed. The most common interview tool.
key_concepts: Hash function maps key → bucket index. Collisions handled by chaining or open addressing
Average O(1) for get/set/delete — worst case O(n) with many collisions
HashSet: membership check in O(1). HashMap: key→value in O(1)
Frequency counting: iterate once, store counts — classic O(n) pattern
Two-sum pattern: for each x, check if (target - x) is in the map
when_to_use: Need O(1) lookup — 'have I seen this before?'
Grouping elements by a computed key (anagram grouping)
common_patterns: Frequency map: count occurrences, then reason about counts
Complement lookup: store seen values, check for target - current
Group by key: defaultdict(list) to bucket items
Sliding window + hash map for substring problems
thinking_questions: What happens to HashMap performance when load factor exceeds 0.75? What does Java do?
How would you design a HashMap from scratch? What's the tradeoff between array size and collision rate?

ds_concepts.title: Two Pointers
ds_concepts.summary: Two indices moving through an array. Eliminates the need for O(n²) nested loops in sorted arrays.
key_concepts: Classic: left at 0, right at n-1, move inward based on condition
Requires sorted array (usually) — sort first if needed at O(n log n) cost
Fast/slow pointer variant: detect cycles, find midpoints in linked lists
Three-sum: fix one element, run two-pointer on the rest
Deduplication: skip duplicates by checking prev before advancing
when_to_use: Array is sorted and you need pairs/triplets summing to target
Need to compare elements from both ends
Fast/slow pointer for cycle detection or midpoint
common_patterns: Squeeze inward: left++ if sum too small, right-- if too large
Same-direction: both pointers move right, one faster (sliding window)
Fast/slow (Floyd's): cycle detection, nth-from-end in linked list
ds_concepts.code_example:
# Two-sum in sorted array O(n)
def two_sum_sorted(nums, target):
    l, r = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while l < r:
        s = nums[l] + nums[r]
        if s == target: return [l, r]
        elif s < target: l += 1
        else: r -= 1

# Three-sum O(n²)
def three_sum(nums):
    nums.sort()
    res = []
    for i, n in enumerate(nums):
        if i > 0 and n == nums[i-1]: continue  # skip duplicates
        l, r = i+1, len(nums)-1
        while l < r:
            s = n + nums[l] + nums[r]
            if s == 0: res.append([n, nums[l], nums[r]]); l += 1
            elif s < 0: l += 1
            else: r -= 1
    return res
thinking_questions: Why does two-pointer only work reliably on sorted arrays for sum problems?
How does Floyd's cycle detection work — why does fast pointer catching slow guarantee a cycle?

ds_concepts.title: Sliding Window
ds_concepts.summary: A subarray window that expands/contracts as it slides. Converts O(n²) substring/subarray problems to O(n).
key_concepts: Fixed window: window size k stays constant, slide right dropping leftmost
Variable window: expand right, shrink from left when constraint violated
Maintain window state (sum, freq map, distinct count) incrementally
Key insight: when you move window right, you only add one element and remove one
At most K distinct: classic variable window with a hashmap tracking frequencies

time_complexity.variable_window: O(n) — each element added/removed at most once

when_to_use: Maximum/minimum sum subarray of size k
Anagram or permutation detection in string
common_patterns: Expand right always, shrink left when constraint violated
Track window state with a Counter/HashMap
Result = max(result, r - l + 1) after each valid expansion
thinking_questions: Why is the variable sliding window O(n) even though it has a nested while loop to shrink?
How would you modify the window approach to find all anagrams of a pattern in a string?

ds_concepts.title: Stack
ds_concepts.summary: LIFO. Push/pop in O(1). The monotonic stack is one of the most powerful interview patterns.
key_concepts: LIFO: last in, first out. Push and pop from same end
Call stack analogy: used for DFS, backtracking, expression evaluation
Monotonic stack: maintain increasing or decreasing order by popping on violation
Monotonic increasing stack: each element is larger than all below it
Classic uses: next greater element, largest rectangle in histogram, daily temperatures
when_to_use: Next greater/smaller element to the right or left
common_patterns: Monotonic decreasing stack: pop when current > top → finds next greater element
Monotonic increasing stack: pop when current < top → finds next smaller element
Min stack: maintain auxiliary stack tracking minimum at each level
thinking_questions: Why is a monotonic stack O(n) even though there's a while loop inside a for loop?
How does the 'largest rectangle in histogram' problem use a monotonic stack? What does the stack track?

ds_concepts.title: Binary Search
ds_concepts.summary: Halve the search space each step. O(log n). Works on any monotonic function, not just sorted arrays.
key_concepts: Standard: find target in sorted array by comparing mid to target
Template: lo=0, hi=n-1, mid=(lo+hi)//2, move lo or hi based on condition
Off-by-one: lo<=hi (find exact) vs lo<hi (find boundary)
Search on answer: binary search the answer space when you can verify feasibility
Rotated array: determine which half is sorted, then decide which half to search
when_to_use: Search on answer: 'minimum/maximum X such that condition holds'
Koko eating bananas, ship packages — classic search-on-answer
common_patterns: Find leftmost: when arr[mid]==target, move hi=mid-1 (keep searching left)
Find rightmost: when arr[mid]==target, move lo=mid+1
Search on answer: define feasible(mid), binary search over answer range
ds_concepts.code_example:
# Standard binary search
def binary_search(nums, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if nums[mid] == target: return mid
        elif nums[mid] < target: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid - 1
    return -1

# Search on answer — minimum eating speed (Koko)
def min_eating_speed(piles, h):
    def can_finish(k):
        return sum((p + k - 1) // k for p in piles) <= h
    lo, hi = 1, max(piles)
    while lo < hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if can_finish(mid): hi = mid  # try smaller
        else: lo = mid + 1
    return lo
thinking_questions: Why do we use mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2 instead of (lo + hi) // 2 in some languages?
How do you binary search for the first element >= target (lower_bound) in a sorted array?

ds_concepts.title: Linked Lists
ds_concepts.summary: Nodes with pointers. O(1) insert/delete at known position. Master the dummy head and fast/slow pointer.
key_concepts: Singly linked: each node has val + next. No random access — must traverse O(n)
Dummy head node: simplifies edge cases for insertion/deletion at the head
Fast/slow pointer: fast moves 2x speed. When fast reaches end, slow is at midpoint
Cycle detection: fast/slow meet inside cycle → Floyd's algorithm
Reversal: iterative with prev/curr/next pointers. Recursive also works
In-place operations: always draw pointer diagrams — order of assignments matters

time_complexity.insert_at_tail: O(n) singly / O(1) if tail pointer maintained

when_to_use: Midpoint, nth from end, cycle detection
common_patterns: Dummy head: prev = ListNode(0); prev.next = head — handles head deletion cleanly
Fast/slow: midpoint, cycle, nth-from-end
Reverse in-place: prev=None, while curr: next=curr.next; curr.next=prev; prev=curr; curr=next
ds_concepts.code_example:
# Reverse linked list — iterative O(n)
def reverse_list(head):
    prev = None
    curr = head
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt
    return prev

# Find middle using fast/slow
def find_middle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    return slow  # slow is at midpoint

# Remove nth from end — one pass with dummy head
def remove_nth(head, n):
    dummy = ListNode(0, head)
    l, r = dummy, head
    for _ in range(n): r = r.next
    while r:
        l = l.next; r = r.next
    l.next = l.next.next
    return dummy.next
thinking_questions: Why does the dummy head pattern eliminate edge cases for head deletion?
How does the fast/slow pointer guarantee slow is exactly at the midpoint when fast reaches the end?

ds_concepts.title: Binary Trees
ds_concepts.summary: Recursive structure. Every tree problem has a recursive subproblem. Master DFS and BFS traversals.
key_concepts: Height: O(log n) for balanced, O(n) worst case (degenerate/skewed)
DFS traversals: inorder (L→Root→R), preorder (Root→L→R), postorder (L→R→Root)
BFS (level-order): use a queue, process level by level
Recursive pattern: solve for left subtree, solve for right subtree, combine
Return value vs global variable: track max path through node needs global; height can return up
common_patterns: Return (value, bool) tuple upward to propagate multiple facts
Global max variable updated inside DFS for path problems
BFS with deque: process all nodes at each level before moving down
Sentinel None returns for base case: if not node: return 0
thinking_questions: When would you choose BFS over DFS for a tree problem? Give a concrete example.
Why does the max path sum problem need a global variable instead of just returning from recursion?

ds_concepts.title: Heap / Priority Queue
ds_concepts.summary: Complete binary tree stored as array. O(log n) insert/extract. Get min/max in O(1).
key_concepts: Min-heap: parent <= children. heapq in Python is min-heap by default
Max-heap: negate values to simulate with Python's heapq
heappush O(log n), heappop O(log n), heap[0] is always min O(1)
K largest/smallest: use a heap of size k — O(n log k) vs O(n log n) sort
Merge K sorted lists: push (val, list_idx) into heap, pop min and push next from same list
when_to_use: Repeatedly need the minimum or maximum element
K largest / K smallest / Kth largest
Merge K sorted arrays or lists
common_patterns: K smallest: max-heap of size k — pop when size > k (keeps k smallest)
K largest: min-heap of size k — pop when size > k (keeps k largest)
Custom comparator: push (priority, item) tuples
ds_concepts.js_code_example:
// JS has no built-in heap — use a sorted structure or implement
// For interviews: mention O(n log k) approach with a min-heap of size k
thinking_questions: Why is building a heap from an array O(n) and not O(n log n)?
For finding the Kth largest: when do you use a min-heap of size k vs sorting?

ds_concepts.title: Trie (Prefix Tree)
ds_concepts.summary: Tree where each path from root to node represents a prefix. O(L) insert/search where L = word length.
key_concepts: Each node has up to 26 children (one per letter) and an is_end flag
Insert: traverse/create nodes for each char, set is_end=True at last char
Search: traverse nodes — return False if any char missing
StartsWith (prefix search): same as search but don't need is_end
Space: O(n × L) — n words, average length L. Worse than hash but enables prefix ops
common_patterns: TrieNode with children dict and is_end bool
DFS on trie to find all words with a prefix
Word search II: build trie of words, DFS on board checking trie
thinking_questions: When would a Trie be better than a HashSet for a dictionary? When worse?
How would you use a Trie to implement autocomplete that returns the top 3 completions by frequency?

ds_concepts.title: Graphs
ds_concepts.summary: Nodes + edges. BFS for shortest path (unweighted). DFS for connectivity, cycles, topological sort.
key_concepts: Representations: adjacency list O(V+E) space (use this), adjacency matrix O(V²)
BFS: queue, visited set, level-by-level — finds shortest path in unweighted graph
DFS: stack/recursion, visited set — connectivity, cycle detection, topological sort
Union-Find: disjoint set for cycle detection and connected components in O(α(n)) ≈ O(1)
Topological sort: Kahn's (BFS + in-degree) or DFS post-order
when_to_use: Cycle detection → DFS with colors or Union-Find
common_patterns: Visited set or in-place marking (flip cell value) to avoid revisits
BFS from multiple sources simultaneously (multi-source BFS)
DFS with state: WHITE/GRAY/BLACK for cycle detection in directed graphs
thinking_questions: Why does BFS guarantee the shortest path in an unweighted graph but DFS does not?
How does Union-Find detect a cycle when adding edges one by one?

ds_concepts.title: 1D Dynamic Programming
ds_concepts.summary: Break problem into overlapping subproblems. Cache results. Bottom-up tabulation > top-down memoization for space.
key_concepts: Optimal substructure: optimal solution built from optimal sub-solutions
Overlapping subproblems: same subproblem solved repeatedly without DP
Top-down (memoization): recursion + cache. Intuitive to write, O(n) space call stack
Bottom-up (tabulation): iterate, fill dp array. Usually O(1) space with rolling variable
State definition is everything: dp[i] = 'what does this represent?'

time_complexity.typical_1d_dp: O(n) time, O(n) space or O(1) with optimization

when_to_use: Count ways to reach a goal
common_patterns: Climbing stairs: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] — only need last 2 values
House robber: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i])
LIS: dp[i] = max(dp[j]+1 for j<i where nums[j]<nums[i]) — O(n²), or patience sort O(n log n)
thinking_questions: How do you identify if a problem has overlapping subproblems vs just recursion?
When can you optimize a DP solution from O(n) space to O(1)? What's the pattern?

ds_concepts.title: 2D Dynamic Programming
ds_concepts.summary: State depends on two dimensions. Grid paths, string matching (edit distance, LCS), knapsack.
key_concepts: dp[i][j] represents some value at position (i,j) in a 2D state space
Grid paths: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1] (unique paths)
String matching: dp[i][j] involves comparing s1[i] with s2[j]
Edit distance: dp[i][j] = min insert/delete/replace at each char pair
Knapsack: dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with capacity w

time_complexity.typical_2d_dp: O(n×m) time, O(n×m) space or O(min(n,m)) with row optimization

common_patterns: Base cases: first row = 0..n, first col = 0..m for edit distance
LCS: if chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1; else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
Space optimization: roll current row over previous row
thinking_questions: How do you reduce edit distance from O(n×m) space to O(min(n,m))? What observation enables this?
What's the difference between longest common subsequence and longest common substring?

ds_concepts.title: Backtracking
ds_concepts.summary: Explore all possibilities by trying choices and undoing them. Prune branches early to avoid brute force.
key_concepts: Template: choose → explore → unchoose (undo)
State space tree: each node is a partial solution, leaves are complete solutions
Pruning: if current state can't lead to valid solution, return early
Permutations vs combinations: permutations care about order, combinations don't
Distinct results: sort + skip duplicates at the same recursion level
when_to_use: Generate all permutations, combinations, or subsets
common_patterns: Path array: build up path, add to results at base case, pop on backtrack
Used set: track which indices are used in current path (permutations)
Start index: pass start=i+1 to avoid reuse in combinations
ds_concepts.code_example:
# Subsets — O(2^n)
def subsets(nums):
    res, path = [], []
    def backtrack(start):
        res.append(path[:])
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            path.append(nums[i])
            backtrack(i + 1)
            path.pop()  # undo
    backtrack(0)
    return res

# Permutations — O(n!)
def permutations(nums):
    res, path = [], []
    used = [False] * len(nums)
    def backtrack():
        if len(path) == len(nums): res.append(path[:]); return
        for i in range(len(nums)):
            if not used[i]:
                used[i] = True; path.append(nums[i])
                backtrack()
                used[i] = False; path.pop()
    backtrack()
    return res
thinking_questions: What's the difference between generating subsets vs combinations vs permutations in terms of the recursion structure?
How do you avoid duplicate subsets when the input array has duplicate values?

ds_concepts.title: Bit Manipulation
ds_concepts.summary: Work directly on binary representations. O(1) tricks for even/odd, powers of 2, XOR uniqueness.
key_concepts: AND (&): both bits 1. OR (|): either bit 1. XOR (^): bits differ. NOT (~): flip bits
Left shift (<<): multiply by 2. Right shift (>>): divide by 2
n & (n-1): clears the lowest set bit (counts 1-bits, checks power of 2)
n & 1: checks if odd. n ^ n = 0: XOR with itself cancels
XOR trick: a ^ b ^ b = a — XOR with same value twice cancels out
when_to_use: Find single non-duplicate in array where all others appear twice → XOR all
common_patterns: XOR all elements: duplicates cancel, unique element remains
Bitmask subsets: for mask in range(1<<n) — iterates all 2^n subsets
Lowest set bit: n & (-n) isolates the rightmost 1-bit
ds_concepts.code_example:
# Single number — XOR O(n) time O(1) space
def single_number(nums):
    result = 0
    for n in nums:
        result ^= n  # duplicates cancel: a^a=0, 0^b=b
    return result

# Count set bits (Hamming weight)
def count_bits(n):
    count = 0
    while n:
        n &= n - 1  # clear lowest set bit
        count += 1
    return count

# Check if power of 2
def is_power_of_two(n):
    return n > 0 and (n & (n-1)) == 0

# Generate all subsets using bitmask
def all_subsets(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    return [[nums[j] for j in range(n) if mask & (1<<j)]
            for mask in range(1<<n)]
thinking_questions: Why does n & (n-1) clear the lowest set bit? Walk through the binary arithmetic.
How would you find two non-duplicate numbers in an array where all others appear twice, using XOR?

ds_concepts.title: Greedy Algorithms
ds_concepts.summary: Make the locally optimal choice at each step. Works when greedy choice property and optimal substructure hold — no backtracking needed.
key_concepts: Greedy choice property: local optimum leads to global optimum
Optimal substructure: optimal solution built from optimal subproblems
Sorting first often enables greedy (intervals, scheduling, gas station)
Two heaps / priority queues enable greedy on dynamic sets
Exchange argument: proof technique — show swapping any two elements worsens result

time_complexity.typical: O(n log n) — dominated by sort
time_complexity.with_heap: O(n log k) for heap-based greedy

when_to_use: Interval scheduling: maximize non-overlapping intervals → sort by end time
Jump Game: can you reach end? → track max reachable index
Gas Station: can you complete circuit? → running sum trick
Meeting Rooms: how many rooms needed? → sort start, min-heap of end times
Task Scheduler: idle time minimization → frequency-based greedy
common_patterns: Sort by end time (interval problems): greedy picks earliest-ending valid interval
Sort by start time + merge: combine overlapping intervals
Two-pass greedy: left pass + right pass (Candy, Trap Rain Water)
Greedy with heap: always process min/max of current set
thinking_questions: Why does sorting by end time (not start time) work for interval scheduling maximization?
How do you distinguish when to use greedy vs dynamic programming? What property does greedy require that DP doesn't?

ds_concepts.title: Intervals
ds_concepts.summary: Problems involving ranges [start, end]. Key operations: merge overlapping, detect conflicts, insert into sorted list, find minimum coverage.
key_concepts: Two intervals [a,b] and [c,d] overlap if a <= d AND c <= b
Sort by start time to process intervals left to right
Merge: if cur.start <= last.end → extend last.end = max(last.end, cur.end)
Meeting rooms: count max overlapping at any point → sort start+end separately
Insert interval: binary search or linear scan to find where new interval fits
when_to_use: Merge overlapping intervals into minimum set
Insert a new interval into sorted non-overlapping list
Check if any intervals overlap (meeting conflict detection)
common_patterns: Sort by start, iterate and merge: result[-1][1] = max(result[-1][1], cur[1])
Two-array trick: separate starts[] and ends[], sort both, scan with two pointers
Sweep line: events at start (+1) and end (-1), find max active count
thinking_questions: What's the edge case when two intervals share exactly one endpoint — do they overlap?
For minimum meeting rooms, why does the two-pointer approach on sorted starts and ends work?

ds_concepts.title: Math & Geometry
ds_concepts.summary: Number theory, geometry, and simulation problems. Key patterns: modulo arithmetic, prime sieve, GCD, spiral/rotation matrix, digit manipulation.
key_concepts: Modulo: (a * b) % m == ((a % m) * (b % m)) % m — prevents overflow in large computations
GCD (Euclidean): gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a % b), base case gcd(a, 0) = a
Prime sieve (Sieve of Eratosthenes): O(n log log n) to find all primes up to n
Matrix rotation 90°: transpose then reverse each row
Spiral traversal: shrink boundaries (top, bottom, left, right) layer by layer
Digit extraction: n % 10 gives last digit, n // 10 removes it
when_to_use: Rotate image / spiral matrix → boundary simulation
Happy number / detect cycle → fast/slow pointer on digit transform
Reverse integer / palindrome number → digit extraction loop
Power(x, n) → fast exponentiation O(log n)
common_patterns: Fast power: x^n = x^(n/2) * x^(n/2) — halve exponent each step
Cycle detection via hash set: seen = set(), loop until n in seen or n == 1
Layer-by-layer simulation: four-pointer bounds shrinking for matrix problems
thinking_questions: Why does transpose + reverse-row equal a 90° clockwise rotation? Verify with a 3x3 example.
In the Sieve of Eratosthenes, why do we start marking multiples at i*i instead of 2*i?

ds_concepts.title: Advanced Graphs
ds_concepts.summary: Algorithms beyond basic BFS/DFS: shortest paths (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford), minimum spanning trees (Kruskal, Prim), topological sort, and union-find.
key_concepts: Dijkstra: min-heap BFS for shortest path in weighted graphs — O((V+E) log V), no negative weights
Bellman-Ford: relax all edges V-1 times — O(VE), handles negative weights, detects negative cycles
Topological Sort: Kahn's algorithm (in-degree BFS) or DFS post-order for DAG ordering
Union-Find (DSU): path compression + union by rank — near O(1) amortized per operation
Kruskal's MST: sort edges by weight, union-find to avoid cycles — O(E log E)
Prim's MST: grow tree greedily from source using min-heap — O(E log V)
when_to_use: Shortest path with weights, no negatives → Dijkstra
Shortest path with negative weights / cycle detection → Bellman-Ford
Course scheduling, build order, task dependencies → Topological Sort
Connected components, cycle detection, dynamic connectivity → Union-Find
Minimum cost to connect all nodes → MST (Kruskal or Prim)
common_patterns: Dijkstra: heap = [(0, src)]; dist = {src: 0}; expand cheapest unvisited
Topological sort via in-degree: queue all nodes with in-degree 0, decrement neighbors
Union-Find with path compression: parent[find(x)] collapses chain to root
Detect cycle in directed graph: DFS with 3 states (unvisited, visiting, visited)
thinking_questions: Why can't Dijkstra handle negative edge weights? What goes wrong when you relax a node that's already been 'settled'?
In topological sort, how do you detect if the graph has a cycle using Kahn's algorithm?
